coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on PIM-1 semiconducting polymer dots for turn-off-on sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Feizi, Foroozan
Molaabasi, Fatemeh
Shamsipur, Hosna
Mousavi, Farimah
Sedghi, Mosslim
Budd, Peter
Naderi-Manesh, Hosein
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a simple green platform based on a novel type of polymer dots (Pdots) as a sensitive and rapid “off-on” fluorescent probe for ratiometric Fe3+ and Cu2+ detection. The ratiometric probe has been designed using green-emitting PIM-1 Pdots and red-emitting PFTBT Pdots as detecting probes and internal stable standard probes, respectively. The dual-emission characteristics of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots allowed us to apply ratiometric ion determination based on aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of PIM-1 Pdots at 490 nm, while the red emission intensity kept highly stable. The role of porosity and surface carboxylic groups of PIM-1 to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity using quantum-mechanical calculation and cellular imaging was successfully demonstrated so that the detection limit of 7.4 nM for Fe3+ and 14.8 nM for Cu2+ was obtained. The “on” process with excellent selectivity was realized by the introducing of glutathione (GSH) into PIM-1/PFTBT/Cu2+ and Cytochrome c (Cyt c) into PIM-1/PFTBT/Fe3+ with fluorescence recoveries of the blank sample 92 % and 91 %, respectively. The proposed strategy detects GSH and Cyt c with a good linearity ranging from 300 nM to 1800 nM and 10 nM to 120 nM by the limit of detection of 85.3 nM and 0.6 nM, respectively. This combinational off-on strategy provides a promising nanosensing platform for monitoring Fe3+ and Cu2+ in the environmental samples and introduces a sensitive probe with high biocompatibility for intracellular target imaging.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on PIM-1 semiconducting polymer dots for turn-off-on sensing and bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a simple green platform based on a novel type of polymer dots (Pdots) as a sensitive and rapid “off-on” fluorescent probe for ratiometric Fe3+ and Cu2+ detection. The ratiometric probe has been designed using green-emitting PIM-1 Pdots and red-emitting PFTBT Pdots as detecting probes and internal stable standard probes, respectively. The dual-emission characteristics of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots allowed us to apply ratiometric ion determination based on aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of PIM-1 Pdots at 490 nm, while the red emission intensity kept highly stable. The role of porosity and surface carboxylic groups of PIM-1 to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity using quantum-mechanical calculation and cellular imaging was successfully demonstrated so that the detection limit of 7.4 nM for Fe3+ and 14.8 nM for Cu2+ was obtained. The “on” process with excellent selectivity was realized by the introducing of glutathione (GSH) into PIM-1/PFTBT/Cu2+ and Cytochrome c (Cyt c) into PIM-1/PFTBT/Fe3+ with fluorescence recoveries of the blank sample 92 % and 91 %, respectively. The proposed strategy detects GSH and Cyt c with a good linearity ranging from 300 nM to 1800 nM and 10 nM to 120 nM by the limit of detection of 85.3 nM and 0.6 nM, respectively. This combinational off-on strategy provides a promising nanosensing platform for monitoring Fe3+ and Cu2+ in the environmental samples and introduces a sensitive probe with high biocompatibility for intracellular target imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: We believe sharing our research data helps other researchers refer to our article with confidence.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper, iron, and zinc are the three most abundant transition metal ions in many organisms. Iron is an essential metal in the human body due to its important role in various metabolic processes including DNA synthesis, electron transport, and oxygen transport [1–4]. Copper as other important metal in the organism is a relatively low toxic metal and lack of it in the body results in biochemical, structural, and functional alteration [5–7]. However, the deposition of copper in the body and tissue causes liver damage and kidney failure. Most of the copper in the body is present as Cu2+, which could be serve as cofactors in proteins and many of the enzymes [8–10]. Traditional methods to determine the iron and copper content such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) and flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) require complicated sample pretreatment processes and sophisticated instrumentation [11,12]. Therefore, there have been ongoing efforts to develop a rapid, reliable, and economical technique for the accurate determination of iron and copper ions in the environment and biological matrices. In this regard, fluorescence spectroscopy is a high-sensitivity and simplicity method [13–15]. Fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) based on sensors such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), silicon quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs), fluorescent nanoclusters (FNCs), and polymer dots (Pdots) have been paid immense attention to detect various ions [16–22]. Some of their advantages are good sensitivity and selectivity, relatively low cost, and simplicity. Pdots are a fascinating class of nanomaterials that extensively are employed as efficient fluorescent nanoprobes because of their outstanding optical characteristics and specific features including fast emission rate, excellent photostability, tunable particle size, simple modified surface, high brightness, large π -conjugated structures, and freely moving electrons within the polymer backbone through the overlaps of p-orbital clouds occurred by tunneling, hopping, or other related mechanisms [23–25]. The excellent biocompatibility of Pdots compared to QDs and CDs as well as Pdots's high quantum yield than other FNCs are also attractive features for scientists in biological medical applications. The main component of Pdots are fluorescent semiconducting polymers such as poly [9, 9-di-(2′-ethylhexyl) fluorenyl-2, 7-diyl] (PDHF), poly(p-phenylene-ethynylene)(PPE), poly[9,9-dioctylfluorenyl- 2,7-diyl)-co-1,4-benzo-{2,10−3}-thiadiazole)](PFBT), poly[(4-(5-(7-methyl-9,9-dioctyl-9 H-fluoren-2-yl)thiophen-2-yl)− 7-(5-methylthiophen-2-yl)benzo[1,2,5]thiadiazole)](PFTBT).

The polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIM) are belonged porous organic polymers (POPs) category having highly rigid contorted backbone [30,31]. The high rigidity and porosity of Pdots arise from the sites of contortion as spirocenters consisting of two five-membered rings connected by a single carbon atom. Spirocenters of Pdots prevent the structure collapse and filling space. To create a wide range of PIMs, various types of spirocyclic precursors could be utilized in the synthesis process [32,33]. Duo to the large surface area, high porosity, and excellent physicochemical stability, PIM polymers have attracted considerable attention as membrane materials for gas separation [34–37]. The first polymer of this type, namely, PIM-1 ( Scheme 1a), was prepared from two commercially available monomers, the spirocyclic biscatechol 5,50,6,60, -tetrahydroxy-3,3,30,30-tetramethyl-1,10-spirobisindane and tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile. PIM-1 can absorb and emit visible light due to the existence of aromatic molecules and two electrons pulling cyano groups in its structure. Scheme 1b, shows the facilitated model of the fluorescent structural unit constituting PIM-1 [38]. Shaouhua Chen et al. investigated the effect of the porosity of a PIM-1 on its intrinsic fluorescence. The fluorescence quenching experiments demonstrated that the high porosity of a PIM-1 matrix is matched by the high accessibility of fluorophore units for quenchers [38]. To date, the fluorescence studies on PIM-1 have focused on its intrinsic fluorescence directly in organic solvents or as a membrane immersed in organic solution. The results demonstrated that PIM-1 whether in solution or a membrane, could be quenched in a similar manner, either by proton donor or electron-rich type of quencher. This supports the idea that PIM-1 chains constituting the membrane are well exposed to the solvent as a result of the high porosity of the PIM-1 membrane [38]. It is noted that, although many papers are available in relation to the Pdots, no reports have been published for the use of PIM-1 as a fluorescent probe in biosensing so far.

Glutathione (GSH) is one of the essential thiolated tripeptides present in eukaryotic cells, its function is to protect cellular components from oxidation by reactive oxygen species. In this perspective, monitoring the change of GSH concentration in the cell has attracted much attention. Hence, different techniques such as chemical high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), mass spectrometry (MS), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and electrochemical method have been used to GSH detection. More recently, fluorescent-based probes have been developed to rapid and direct detection of GSH due to some limitations of traditional detection methods such as the costly equipment required, low productivity, and complicated procedures [39]. In recent years, ratiometric fluorescent probes have attracted much attention owing to their advantages. Ratiometric fluorescence design improves the reliability and sensitivity of probes by removing some notorious properties such as photobleaching of the fluorophore, light scattering caused by the sample matrix, fluctuations in the excitation source, variations in the local concentration of probe, and the effect of microenvironment around the probe [40–42]. Moreover, the ratiometric strategy is appropriate for visual detection, this technique relies on changes in color intensity, color brightness, or color tonality, which is used for detecting a target analyte [43,44].

The ratiometric fluorescence sensors based nanomaterials are much preferred for high sensitivity and biosensing capability. The commonly designed ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which have been reported before are based on single-target sensing [45–47], efforts are underway to introduce a method to generate biocompatibility fluorescence ratiometric nano sensors with biosensing capability for multi-target sensing[48,49].

Herein, a novel biocompatibility ratiometric fluorescent design has been developed using PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots in which PIM-1 Pdots is employed for detecting signal and PFTBT Pdots could act as an internal stable standard. This probe with outstanding properties such as single-wavelength excitation, self-calibration, biocompatibility, and visual detection capability lead us to suggest that PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots can be used to multi-target sensing and cell imaging. We successfully demonstrated that the intrinsic porous structure of PIM-1 Pdots with carboxyl functional groups on its surface plays an important role to design fluorescence turn off-on probes. In this regard, the developed system displayed a highly selective and sensitive “turn off” fluorescence response toward Fe3+ and Cu2+. Moreover, we demonstrated that after the addition of GSH into PIM-1-PFTBT/Cu2+, the fluorescence of the probe solution could be turned on by the complex formation between GSH peptide and Cu2+ ions, leading to an excellent sensitivity and selectivity for GSH determination. Finally, the probe was successfully employed for the determination of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in real water samples and GSH in serum samples as well as cell imaging application.

Anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF≥99.9 %, inhibitor-free), polystyrene-co-maleic anhydride (PSMA), PFTBT were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Cu (NO3)2, FeCl30.6 H2O, and other routine reagents were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Cytochrome c (Cyt c) and l-glutathione reduced (GSH) are both from Sigma-Aldrich. The fluorescence spectra were measured with a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp source. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with an Agilent 8453 Diode Array Spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted on a Bruker infrared spectrophotometer and a Metrohm pH Meter 713 was applied to read the pH values.

PIM-1 was synthesized by polycondensation of TTSBI and TFTPN according to the former literature [50]. Both functionalized PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots were prepared by a co-precipitation method. In the first step, 1 mg/mL stock solution of PIM-1, PFTBT, and PSMA in THF was prepared. In the second step, to functionalize PIM-1 with –COOH groups, 20 µL of PIM-1 and 10 µL of PSMA were added to 1 mL of THF. This mixture was sonicated for 3 min to obtain a homogeneous solution and then was quickly injected into the 3 mL deionized water under intense sonication. Then THF was removed by the stream of nitrogen gas at 80 ºC. To synthesize PFTBT Pdots, 40 µL of stock solution of PFTBT was added to 1 mL of THF, and this mixture was quickly injected into the 2 mL deionized water under intense sonication in a bath sonicator. THF was then removed by the stream of nitrogen gas at 80 °C. The ratiometric fluorescent probe was prepared by mixing the functionalized PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots with the fluorescent intensity ratio of roughly 1:1 in 2 mL phosphate buffer (pH=7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of metal ions and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 450–850 nm upon excitation at 380 nm. The excitation and emission slits were both set at 5 nm. The ion sensing experiments were performed as follows. For all measurements, 2 mL of the ratiometric fluorescent probe solution (2.6 µg/mL) was mixed with various concentrations of Fe3+ (0–1000 nM) and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after 2 min. Similar procedures were performed in the presence of Cu2+ (0–1500 nM). To detect GSH based on the turn-on experiments, GSH solution (0–1800 nM) was added to PIM-1-PFTBT/Cu2+ solution and the fluorescence intensity was recorded.

The computational models were drawn and geometrically optimized by Merck Molecular Force Field (MMFF94) [51,52] and by using the Spartan ‘16 package [53]. Further, the single-point calculation with the parameterized hybrid of ab-initio (restricted Hartree-Fock) and semi-empirical quantum mechanical (QM) method, so-called RHF/PM6 [54], were applied to confirm the binding mode and the molecular orbital (MO) energy diagram of the PIM-1/PSMA model and its interaction with Cu2+ and Fe3+ cations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: For drink water sample, it was mixed with Fe3+ and Cu2+ standard solutions, and the mixtures were ready for further analysis. In the case of lake water, samples were pretreated with ordinary qualitative filter paper (0.2 mm) to remove the insoluble substances. Then, different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were added to the pretreated water samples and detected with the fluorescent probe solution. The average response was obtained from three independent experiments, and so then the standard deviation was calculated. As well as detection of GSH was carried out in serum samples. The serum was spiked by GSH with different concentrations (500, 1000, 1500 nM).

The toxicity of PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots for Hela cells was investigated by the standard MTT assay. Hela cells were seeded into the 96-well plate at the density of 1.0 × 105 per mL to confluence overnight. Then, the cell medium was removed and treated with DMEM medium containing the PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots with various concentrations (2.5–50 µL/mL) for another 24 h. After that, the cells were pulsed with 20 µL of MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS) for 4 h. Then, 100 µL DMSO was added to each well. control wells contain only DMSO without PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots. Finally, the absorption was recorded at the wavelength of 570 nm.

For fluorescence cell imaging, cells were cultured into a 24-well culture plate (SPL Life Sciences Co., Ltd. Korea) in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 humidified incubator to reach a good confluency. To ensure probe uptake, at first HeLa cells were incubated with PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots, individually and used as controls. For this reason, 2 mL DMEM containing PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots probe was added to the cells at 37 ºC for 6 h. After that, cells were washed twice with PBS, and fluorescent images were acquired. Then 0.5, 1.0, and 5.0 µM Cu2+ solutions were added and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Finally, the cells were imaged again after washing three times with PBS using an inverted fluorescence microscope equipped with blue and green filters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization, and stability of Pdots
ce:section.ce:para: A new ratiometric fluorescence probe was successfully synthesized by mixing green fluorescent PIM-1 Pdots and red fluorescent PFTBT Pdots. It is worth mentioning that in the designed ratiometric fluorescence probe, PFTBT Pdots serve as a good control in the experiment. During PIM-1 Pdot formation, the hydrophobic polystyrene portions of PSMA co-polymers tended to be embedded inside the Pdot matrix while the maleic anhydride unit is hydrolyzed, producing free –COOH groups extended outside of PIM-1 Pdots into the aqueous environment. Hence, surfaces of as-synthesized PIM-1 Pdots contain a large number of –COOH ligands which can coordinate with Fe3+ and Cu2+ metallic ions by the surface complexation reaction to form a chelate, leading to the fluorescence quenching. To further investigate, the FT-IR analysis was carried out to characterize the surface mainly functional groups of the PIM-1 and prepared PIM-1 Pdots. As indicated in Fig. 1a, the main absorption peaks for PIM-1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C-CN bond around 2241 cm−1 and the aromatic ether linkage near 1264 cm−1. The obvious strong peak around 1452 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of the CN group and a peak at 1629 cm−1 mainly originated from the CC aromatic stretching vibration. The strong peak around 2865 cm−1 and 2958 cm−1 could be related to the –CH3 stretching vibration. The obvious strong peak around 1630 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of PIM-1 Pdots belonged to the stretching vibrations of the carboxyl functional groups which originating from the PSMA co-polymer, indicating the presence of –COOH groups on the surface of PIM-1Pdots [55].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots are shown in Fig. 1b. PIM-1 Pdots show three absorption peaks, with a strong emission peak centered at 506 nm. Two absorption bands centered at 232 and 300 nm are corresponding to π → π * transition of CC and n → π * transition of CO, respectively. The absorption peak at 430 nm is assigned to the n-π * and π-π * transitions due to the CO bond and aromatic fluorophore structure, respectively. The wavelengths of the absorption peak and the excitation spectral peak of the PIM-1 Pdots are very close, demonstrating little non-emitting absorption in the PIM-1 Pdots [56]. PFTBT absorption spectra exhibited two distinct peaks at 380 and 520 nm, which could be attributed to the n-π * and the π-π * transitions from the aromatic polymer structure [57]. The maximum emission band of PFTBT Pdots is located at 630 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra of PIM-1 Pdots, PFTBT Pdots, and the mixed PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm have been shown in Fig. 1c. The prepared Pdots exhibited a dual emission spectrum in which one of the emission bands is located at shorter wavelength of 491 nm (green region) and the other is located at a longer wavelength of 630 nm (red region). Compared to PIM-1 Pdots alone, the emission spectrum of PIM-1 in a mixture of PIM-1/PFTB Pdots has ∼12 nm blue shift which is caused by the hybridization effect [58,59]. This is referred to the widening of the bandgap in the molecular orbital diagram of PIM-1 Pdots which might be due to its interaction with PFTBT Pdots. We may assume that the PIM-1 Pdots receive energy from the PFTBT Pdots in the resulting mixture which is responsible for the bandgap widening and blue shift in the fluorescence spectrum of PIM-1 Pdots [60]. The dual emission characteristics make the potential applications of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots in ratiometric detection. To complete the characterization of prepared Pdots, TEM images were recorded (Fig. 1d and e). As can be seen from TEM images, the prepared PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots both display uniform particles with an average diameter size of approximately 25 nm and 50 nm, respectively.

The elemental composition of PIM-1, PFTBT, PIM-1 Pdots, and PFTBT Pdots was evaluated by elemental EDX mapping. As expected, the EDX spectrum of PIM-1 Pdots had no significant change compared to PIM-1, and both samples have same composition (Fig. S1a and b). The concentration of C, N, and O in the PIM-1 Pdots composition showed small changes, which resulted from the incorporating PSMA into PIM-1 Pdots. Furthermore, the EDX spectra of the PFTBT polymer and unmodified PFTBT Pdots did not show any difference (Fig. S1c and d), both samples showed the same content of C, N, and S in their elemental composition. The results of EDX mapping for PIM-1, PIM-1 Pdots, PFTBT, and PFTBT Pdots are shown in Fig. S1e.

Besides, elemental analyses (Table S1) of modified PIM-1 Pdots indicated a low increase in the C/N and C/H ratios and decreases in N/H ratio compared to PIM-1. As mention above incorporating PSMA into synthesis of PIM-1 Pdots leads to low alteration in C/N, C/H, and N/H ratios. In contrast, in the case of PFTBT and PFTBT Pdots, no changes in the elemental composition were observed, which confirms the elemental composition of the polymer does not change during the Pdots synthesis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric methods have some advantages in comparison to a single emission wavelength such as eliminating any interference from the environment or instrument, the fluctuation of the excitation source, and the concentration of probe[61–63]. Our as-synthesized PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots have dual emission bands in the green and red regions. Therefore, we have successfully used these mixing Pdots for ratiometric determination of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by plotting the ratio of the emission intensities of PIM-1 to PFTBT as a function of metallic ions concentration. For this reason, at first, the experimental conditions such as response time and pH for detection of desired ions were optimized. The investigation of the response time of the Pdots to Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions confirms a fast quenching kinetics (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2a and b, the fluorescence quenching is reached a fixed value and the curves become flat after 2 min. Therefore 2 min was adopted as a favorable reaction time for the rapid detection of both Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions. To realize suitable pH, the fluorescent intensity ratio F491/F630 of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots in the presence of a fixed concentration of both Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions at pH 2–10 were individually studied. As shown in Fig. S2c and d, strongly acidic or alkaline pH exerts negative influences on the response of the probe because of the protonation or deprotonation effect in these environments. Comprehensively, the most favorable pH was set at pH= 7, which is where maximum fluorescence quenching occurs.

Next, the fluorescence spectra of Pdots with different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were recorded under optimal conditions. After the introduction of Fe3+ into the PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots solution, the emission intensity of Pdots at 491 nm was drastically quenched while red emission intensity kept highly stable. As shown in Fig. 2a, the emission intensity of the probe is a function of Fe3+ concentration, so that fluorescence quenching was observed with increasing Fe3+ concentrations up to 700 nM, indicating that all of the –COOH groups have been occupied by Fe3+ ions. Significantly, the fluorescent intensity ratio I491/I630 remarkably reduced from the initial 0.899042:1–0.299827:1 at 1000 nM Fe3+ ions, suggesting its ultra-sensitivity of the designed ratiometric probe to Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 2b, it is obvious that a linear range for Fe3+ was obtained from 25 nM to 700 nM with a regression equation of (F0/F) = 0.0013 [Fe3+] + 0.9894. The detection limit was evaluated and calculated at 7.4 nM based on equation LOD = 3S0/m (n = 3), where S0 is the standard deviation of blank measurements and m is the slope of the linear calibration curve. Similar evolutions were also observed after the addition of Cu2+ ions into the PIM-1/PFTBT solution. As shown in Fig. 2c, the fluorescence intensity of the designed probe was closely related to the concentration of Cu2+. From Fig. 2d, it is seen that for a range from 50 to 1400 nM, the curve rises linearly with a regression equation of (F0/F) = 0.0007 [Cu2+] + 0.9977, and for this fitting, the correlation coefficient (R2) is obtained as 0.9904. The LOD is calculated using a lower concentration of the linear portion of the calibration curve and was calculated to be 14.8 nM. Fig. 2e, shows the visual fluorescence emissions of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots to Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions. Upon the addition of Fe3+ or Cu2+ ions, the color of the PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots solution turned from dark orange to red under a 365 nm UV lamp.

To verify the selectivity of PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots toward various metal ions, the fluorescent intensity ratio F491/F630 of PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots with different metal ions was measured. As shown in Fig. 2f, other metal ions exert little effect on the fluorescence quenching, which indicates that the PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots have a good selectivity to Fe3+ and Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “on” of PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots/Cu2+ and PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots/Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the fluorescence restoration of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots/Cu2+ solution, some biological molecules, such as glutamic acid (Glu), tryptophan (Trp), methionine (Met), glutathione (GSH), arginine (Arg), cysteine (Cys), leucine (Leu), tyrosine (Tyr), proline (Pro), asparagine (Asn), and histidine (His) were added to the solution of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots/Cu2+ under the same conditions ( Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. 3b, the various biological molecules did not any have obvious fluorescent enhancement, only GSH caused the restoration fluorescence.

GSH is a tripeptide, consisting of the amino acids l-cysteine, l-glutamic acid, and glycine, which is known for its strong ability to bind with heavy metal ions [64]. Upon the addition of GSH, sandwich complexes were formed between GSH and Cu2+ ions [65]. Indeed, the addition of GSH would break the PIM-1 Pdots/Cu2+ interaction, so that the “off” state of fluorescence of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots/Cu2+ was recovered. The recovery of fluorescence of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots solution in the presence of 1.5 µM Cu2+ is measured upon adding GSH. As shown in Fig. 3c, the quenched fluorescence intensity is recovered to 92 % of blank fluorescence intensity. It was seen the fluorescent emissions of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots/Cu2+ were increased gradually by increasing concentrations of GSH with a good linearity ranging from 300 nM to 1800 nM. We also found a linear correlation between the fluorescent intensity ratio I491/I630 and the GSH concentration such that GSH can be detected at nanomolar concentrations with the LOD of 85.3 nM. The fitted equation is (F0/F) = 0.0015 [GSH] + 1.0856 with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9904 Fig. 3d.

We also monitor the change in the fluorescence intensity of the PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots in the reaction system containing both GSH and Cu2+ ions. As shown in Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity of the PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots have no significant change in the presence of both GSH and Cu2+ (1.5 µM), which resulted from the formation of the sandwich complex between GSH and Cu2+ ions. To address this challenge, an appropriate agent could be used for de-complexation of GSH with Cu2+ ions. Previous studies have demonstrated that GSH has the highest affinity to complexation with Pb2+ ions rather than other heavy metal ions [47,66–68]. As shown in Fig. S4b, fluorescence emission of the PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots in the solution containing both GSH and Cu2+ ions were quenched by introducing 1.5 µM of Pb2+ ions into the reaction system. It was evident that the addition of Pb2+ ions would break the Cu2+/GSH complex and lead to the fluorescence quenching, which could be ascribed to attach Cu2+ ions on the PIM-1 Pdots surfaces and aggregation.

Cyt c with a three-dimensional structure of horse is a hemoprotein composed of ∼ 100 amino acid residues. Among this amino acids fifteen of them namely Cys14, Cys17, His18, Gly29, Pro30, Gly41, Asn52, Trp59, Tyr67, Leu68, Pro71, Pro76, Thr78, Met80, and Phe82 have been incorporated in essential structure and function roles. The iron center is hexacoordinated by the pyrrolic nitrogens of the porphyrin ring and by axial ligands Met80 and His18 of the protein at nature pH [69]. The fluorescence “off” state of PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots/Fe3+ can be restored by adding Cyt c with a good linearity ranging from 10–120 nM. As shown in Fig. 3e, the “off” state of PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots /Fe3+ can be recovered to 91 % of the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample. As shown in Fig. 3f linear correlation between the fluorescent intensity ratio I491/I630 and the Cyt c concentration such that Cyt c can be detected at nanomolar concentrations with LOD of 0.6 nM. The fitted equation is (F0/F) = 0.031 [Cyt c] + 0.9102 with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9958.

The predominant mechanism could be based on aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). Accordingly, abundant carboxyl moieties on the surface of PIM-1 Pdots can selectivity chelate with Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions to form the multivalent and strong coordination between carboxylate and Fe3+ and Cu2+, leading to quenching of the fluorescence of the PIM-1 Pdots. This was followed by recording the single fluorescence response of PIM-1 Pdots at different concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+ where PIM-1 Pdots is employed alone. It was seen that the fluorescent emissions of PIM-1 Pdots were decreased gradually by increasing concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with a good linearity ranging from 25 to 1000 nM and 100–1500 nM for Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively (Fig. S3a and b). The results are expressed by the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. S3c and d). The fitted equation is (F0/F) = 0.0011 [Fe3+] + 1.0686 with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9904 for Fe3+ and (F0/F) = 0.0008 [Cu2+] + 0.9313 with the R2 = 0.9903 for Cu2+.

This phenomenon has also been verified by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta-potential values to determine the surface charge and average diameter of PIM-1 Pdots in the absence and presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+. The hydrodynamic size result shows that the particle size of PIM-1 Pdots increases from 47.7 nm to 279.0 nm and 130.0 nm after the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively ( Fig. 4a, b, and c). This result confirms the aggregation of PIM-1 Pdots in the presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+ metallic ions. Also, the zeta potential studies show that the particle surface of PIM-1 Pdots owns a high negative charge (−15.3 mv), confirming the existence of a large amount of negatively charged carboxylic moieties on the surface of PIM-1 Pdots. As shown in Fig. 4d, the zeta potential of PIM-1 Pdots changes from around − 15.3 mv to around 4.92 mv and − 6.74 mv after the addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. This indicates that Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions have a strong coordinating affinity with the –COOH groups on the surface of PIM-1 Pdots via electrostatic interaction. So, ACQ could be the possible explanation for the observed fluorescence quenching behavior in the PIM-1 Pdots.

According to previous studies [38], PIM-1 is a porous polymer. Therefore, we have investigated the role of porosity in capturing Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions via the surface of PIM-1 Pdots. In this way, we performed the same fluorescence quenching experiment by surface-functionalized PFBT@PSMA Pdots instead of PIM-1 @PSMA Pdots, since PFBT@PSMA Pdots have the same –COOH functional groups without any porosity in their structure. For this reason, the PFBT Pdots were prepared by a similar method of PIM-1 @PSMA Pdots synthesis, while the PFBT polymer is used instead of the PIM-1 polymer as precursor [70]. According to the previous reports [70] in the case of PFBT Pdots, fluorescence quenching was caused by the formation of sandwich complexes between Cu2+ and –COOH groups on the surface of Pdots. As shown in Fig. S4a, the emission intensity drastically decreased with increasing Cu2+ concentrations with linearity ranging from 5 µM to 60 µM Cu2+ ions with a regression equation of (F0/F) = 0.0546 [Cu2+] + 0.8952 Fig. S4b. The LOD was calculated to be 1.30 μM, which is less sensitive than that of PIM-1 Pdots. This confirms well the role of porosity for capturing metallic ions via the surface porosity of PIM-1 Pdots beside carboxylic moieties.

For further investigation, the interaction between Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions with the PIM-1 Pdots was studied by quantum mechanical calculation. First, a large model of PIM-1/PSMA structure was designed consisting of 4 PIM-1 and 16 PSMA monomers. This model with the chemical formula of C116H84N8O16. C192H194O64 was optimized using MMFF94 calculation with further single-point RHF/PM6 semi-empirical parameterization to determine its MO energy diagram ( Fig. 5a). The net binding energy of the model is − 10,360.27 kJ mol−1 and the interactions between PIM-1 and PSMA might be in types of Van der Waals, π-π stacking, and H-bonding between –COOH functional groups of PSMA with the –CN groups of PIM-1 which led to a helix structure in Pdots. The length of hydrogen bonds between PIM-1 and PSMA is also shown in Fig. 5a. Fig. 5b shows a profile of PIM-1 @PSMA Pdots in a larger magnification with the net binding energy of − 5268.74 kJ.mol−1, area of 2517.84 Å2, and volume of 2601.73 Å3. Fig. 5b shows how a linear branch of 8 PSMA monomers wraps around a dimer of PIM-1 with the corresponding MO energy diagram. Finally, the PIM-1 Pdot was used for the determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ cations (Fig. 5c, d). As shown in Fig. 5c, d insets, the cations have been trapped in a space between –COOH functional groups of PSMA and –CN of PIM-1 with mentioned distances in the figure. Table 1 represents the binding energy, the energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO), and the bandgap (ΔEHOMO-LUMO) of the corresponding models. According to Table 1, the PIM-1 Pdot model has a bandgap of 6.31 eV, while the addition of the intermediate metal cations has shrunk it to 5.71 eV and 2.46 eV for Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Furthermore, the value of EHOMO for the PIM-1 Pdots/ Fe3+ model is 0.7 eV lower than PIM-1 Pdots/Cu2+, suggesting a more stable electronic interaction between the MO of the PIM-1 Pdots with Fe3+ compared to Cu2+. This result corresponds well with better sensitivity obtained for Fe3+ compared to Cu2+ (Fig. S3). Fig. 5c and d also present the electrostatic potential maps in the PIM-1 Pdot model while interacting with these two cations. The red areas are defined as negative (lower) electrostatic potentials, while the blue areas show more positive electrostatic potentials. The higher positive charge of Fe3+ has a noticeable impact on the map compared to Cu2+. It was found that the binding energy of PIM-1 Pdots with Cu2+ is 1387.74 kJ mol−1 lower than that with Fe3+, which might provide the higher thermodynamic stability of related Cu2+. However, the same computational study has shown that the interaction of Fe3+ with PIM-1 Pdot was more effective than Cu2+ for both increasing the binding energy and lowering the bandgap values. These significant differences between PIM-1 Pdots/Cu2+ and PIM-1 Pdots/Fe3+ models are responsible for the different optical responses (ACQ phenomena) observed in Fig. S3. Therefore, the computational study recommends the PIM-1 Pdot model as an efficient probe for the determination of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with distinct results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in the reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential application of this sensing system toward the simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions a separate study was carried out. The above results indicated that the fluorescence of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots was quenched in the presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, which resulted from the interaction of ions with the functional groups on PIM-1 Pdot surfaces and aggregation. For the simultaneous detection of two or more targets in the same samples, masking strategy are used to mask one of the target in the presence of the other [71,72]. Based on previous reports, to selective detection of Cu2+ ions ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) could be used as masking agent for masking of Fe3+ [73].

As a predicate to testing the above suggestion, the test samples were prepared by spiking 500 nM of EDTA, into the PIM-1-PFTBT Pdots solutions containing 500 nM of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, individually. As shown in Fig. S6a and b, by adding EDTA the fluorescence intensity of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots/Cu2+ remains almost unchanged, but the quenched fluorescence intensity of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots/Fe3+ was recovered to ∼98 % of its initial value. The concentration of Fe3+ could be calculated from the recovered emission intensity of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots. To be noted, by addition of excessive amounts of EDTA into the solution, no noticeable changes were observed in emission intensity, which suggests that all Fe3+ ions chelated by EDTA. Meanwhile, the Cu2+ concentration could be calculated by comparing the fluorescence intensity of the blank. This measurement showed that the concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in the reaction system containing 500 nM of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions were 505.14 ± 1.05 nM and 498.02 ± 1.48 nM, respectively, which match what was spiked in solution (Fig. S6c). The above result led us to propose that the synthesized probe will be able to detect both ions simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of Cu2+ in real water samples and GSH in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the potential of PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for determining Fe3+ and Cu2+ in real samples, we spiked known amounts of Fe3+ and Cu2+ into pretreated lake water and drink water. As shown in Table 2a, the satisfactory recovery rate for Fe3+ and Cu2+ were changed from 96.18 %∼102.64 % and 92.29∼115.70 %, respectively. The results indicate that the designed ratiometric fluorescent probe could reliably and effectively detect Fe3+ and Cu2+ in actual water samples. As well the proposed ratiometric probe was assessed to detect GSH in human serum samples. As summarized in Table 2b, the recovery values were in the range of 100.4–121.5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence imaging of Cu2+ levels in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: To consider PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots for cellular imaging and clinical applications, cell viability using MTT assay was investigated. In this way, HeLa cell line was chosen and incubated with various concentrations of the as-prepared PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots (2.5–50 µL/mL). As shown in Fig. 6, both PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots displayed low cytotoxic activity and high biocompatibility for HeLa cancer cells at even high concentrations, as the viability remained above ca. 80 % after 24 h incubation time [74]. This reveals that both Pdots with high biocompatibility could be a promising candidate to use in cellular imaging [48]. It is worth mentioning that the slight differences between the cytotoxicity of PIM-1 Pdots and PFTBT Pdots on Hela cells could be attributed to the different cellular uptake of Pdots.

As seen from Fig. S7, loading 40 µL of PIM-1/ PFTBT Pdots into the HeLa cell medium resulted in both green and red fluorescence emissions with high intensity (Fig. S7a). This indicates that the designed nanoprobe based on Pdots possesses suitable cell permeability for live-cell imaging. To further validate the sensing potential of the developed ratiometric probe, then we selected Cu2+ as the target to be tracked in living cells. Interestingly, after incubating the cells with 0.5, 1, and 5 μM Cu2+, the green fluorescence intensity remarkably decreased due to aggregation-caused quenching mechanism (ACQ), while the red fluorescence signal was nearly unchanged upon incubation [75] (Fig. S7c). Moreover, the inhibitory effect of EDTA for Fe3+ and Cu2+ was investigated and found that the green emission of PIM-1 could be restored after the addition of EDTA for cells treated with Fe3+ but not for cells treated with Cu2+ ( Fig. 7a, b, c, d, and e). This is consistent with in vitro solution experiment (Fig. S6), indicating that the interferences between Cu2+ and Fe3+ could be avoided by using EDTA inhibitor, while the red emission of PFTBT probe remains constant. As shown in Fig. 7f, in the case of cells treated with Cu2+ the green emission of PIM-1 Pdots could be restored by addition of GSH, consistent with what was seen for in vitro solution experiments (Fig. 3c). These results demonstrated that the designed PIM-1/ PFTBT Pdots has promising potential to monitor the Cu2+ level in living cells. Therefore, it can be said that the developed PIM-1/PFTBT probe could be extended as an invaluable ratiometric fluorescent probe to use for sensitive intracellular target imaging.

In summary, we prepared a novel sensitive fluorescence ratiometric probe based on PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots. The as-prepared PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots show excellent optical properties with dual-emission spectra (one at 491 nm and the other at 630 nm) under a single wavelength excitation (380 nm). This characteristic allows the developed nanoscale probe to be used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and Cu2+ based on the ACQ mechanism. In this way, the emission intensity of PIM-1 Pdots at 490 nm is quenched as a function of Fe3+ and Cu2+ concentration, while red emission intensities keep highly stable. The result shows that the fluorescent intensity of the ratiometric probe was unaffected or minimally affected by other metals. Consequently, with respect to the porosity and carboxylic groups on the surface of PIM, the designed simple probe showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ and Cu2+. Moreover, the PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots probe was successfully applied in detecting Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions in real water samples and has high potential application in imaging ions in living cells. Moreover, the “turn-on” behavior of designed ratiometric PIM-1/PFTBT/Cu2+ towards GSH and PIM-1/PFTBT/Fe3+ towards Cyt c with high sensitivity and selectivity was successfully demonstrated. Altogether it can be said that the developed PIM-1/PFTBT Pdots provide a promising biocompatible nanoplatforms for turn-off-on sensing and fluorescence cellular bioimaging.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mojtaba Shamsipur received Ph.D. in analytical chemistry at Michigan State University in 1979. He is currently a professor of chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran. His research work has been mainly focused on the thermodynamic and kinetic studies of macrocyclic ligand complexes, and their analytical applications in areas such as ion transport through liquid membranes, solid-phase extraction, ion-selective electrodes, and optical sensors. From the last decade, his research area is also extended to fluorescent nanoparticles and their analytical applications. He has published over 1000 scientific papers in international journals.

Foroozan Feizi received her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran in 2021 under the supervision of Prof. Mojtaba Shamsipur. She is currently a postdoctoral member of Shamsipur’s research group to study about synthesis and characterization of florescent polymer dots and their applications in develop new fluorescenct bio-sensing and bio-imaging systems.

Fatemeh Molaabasi received her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Tabiat Modaress University, Tehran, Iran in 2018 under the supervision of Prof. Mojtaba Shamsipur. She is currently a postdoctoral member of Shamsipur’s research group.
Hosna Shamsipur received her Ph.D. in Organic Chemistry from Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran in 2018. She is currently a postdoctoral member of Peter Budd research group.

Farimah Mousavi received her Ph.D. in NanoChemistry from Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran in 2019 under the supervision of Prof. Mojtaba Shamsipur. She is currently a postdoctoral member of Shamsipur’s research group.

Mosslim Sedghi received her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Tabiat Modaress University, Tehran, Iran in 2018 under the supervision of Prof. Mojtaba Shamsipur. He is currently a postdoctoral member of Naderi-Manesh’s research group.

Peter Budd obtained his first degree (chemistry, 1978) and Ph.D. (1981) from the University of Manchester. He spent 8 years as a research chemist at the BP Research Centre, Sunbury-on-Thames, working on polymers and structural materials, returning to Manchester in 1989 as a lecturer. He is now a Professor of Polymer Chemistry.

Hosein Naderi-Manesh received his Ph.D. in Biophysics from University of California, Berkeley, United States in 1988. He is currently a professor at the faculty of biological sciences at Tabiat Modaress University, Tehran, Iran.
